svg.title: Common progress note mistakes vs stronger approach
svg.desc: Side-by-side comparison of weak and strong psychology progress note language.
svg.text: Common mistakes
Stronger approach
Writing too much narrative
"Client discussed her week at length, mentioning her sister's visit..."
Reads like a transcript. Hard to review, exposes more than necessary.
A note is a clinical record, not a session summary.
Brief and clinical
"Client reported elevated stress following a family visit."
Captures the clinically relevant detail without reproducing
the conversation verbatim.
Writing too little
"Session went well, CBT continued, client coping."
No clinical reasoning, no evidence of progress, no legal protection.
Almost useless if reviewed in an audit or complaint.
Specific and evidenced
"Client applied distress tolerance skills independently during a high-stress week."
Names the skill, demonstrates application, links directly
to treatment goal 2.
No risk documentation
Note is silent on risk entirely
Creates ambiguity if the record is ever reviewed. Silence on risk
is not the same as confirming there were no concerns.
Always address risk explicitly
"No suicidal ideation, self-harm, or risk to others reported or observed."
Even one sentence is sufficient when there are no concerns.
Explicit is always better than absent.
Blurring fact and interpretation
"Client appears hopeless about recovery"
Presents your interpretation as a fact. Creates problems
in legal review and undermines clinical credibility.
Separate fact from interpretation
"Client reported feeling hopeless. Clinician assessed low motivation consistent with..."
What the client said vs what you observed are
two distinct clinical observations.
Vague progress language
"Client is progressing well"
Means almost nothing without observable evidence.
Evidence-based progress
"Demonstrated independent application of skills — consistent with treatment goal 2."
Observable, specific, and tied to a named goal.
